title: Cooking Oil on Cotton
stainChemistry: Cooking oil (vegetable, olive, canola) contains long-chain triglycerides that penetrate cotton rapidly due to low viscosity. Fresh oil is easily dissolved by surfactants. Aged oil oxidizes and polymerizes — especially polyunsaturated oils (canola, soybean) which cross-link into a varnish-like film. Heat accelerates this polymerization.
whyThisWorks: Surfactant (Dawn is the consumer standard) breaks the oil into micelles — tiny droplets surrounded by surfactant molecules that water can carry away from cotton. For polymerized oil, a solvent pre-treatment dissolves the hardened film before surfactant can work. Avoid heat until the oil is fully removed — it polymerizes the residue.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply POG (Paint-Oil-Grease solvent) directly to the stain from the back side where possible. Tamp gently with the bristle end of a spotting brush. POG is a non-polar volatile solvent — it dissolves the triglyceride on contact before water can emulsify and spread it.

If POG alone stops pulling color after 2–3 applications, step up to amyl acetate for stronger solvent action. Apply sparingly — amyl acetate is aggressive on synthetic dyes and softeners. Test an inside seam first on any colored cotton.

Apply NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) to emulsify the remaining oil micro-droplets that the solvent loosened but didn't dissolve. Work into the fiber with a soft brush in gentle circles, then flush with cool water from the back of the fabric.

Dilute 28% acetic acid 1:10 with water and apply to restore pH. Cotton tolerates acid well, but this step is chiefly about neutralizing any residual detergent alkalinity and sharpening the rinse. Follow with a final cool-water flush.

Blot dry with a clean white cloth. Air-dry completely before steam-finishing or pressing. Heat polymerizes any residual triglyceride — if there is any oil shadow left, heat will permanently set it. Inspect in daylight after drying.

steps: 1. Apply POG (Paint Oil Grease remover) directly to the stain. Tamp gently
2. If POG plateaus: apply Amyl Acetate for stronger solvent action
3. Apply NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) to emulsify remaining oil micro-droplets. Flush with cool water
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) for final pH restoration
warnings: Apply solvent BEFORE water — water spreads oil stains
Test amyl acetate on an inside seam before full application on colored cotton

homeSolutions.instruction: Oil and water don't mix. If you rinse a fresh oil stain before anything else, the oil floats and spreads — you end up with a bigger, fainter mark instead of a smaller concentrated one. The order that works is: absorb with powder → work in dry Dawn → warm rinse. Skip the dryer until the stain is completely gone. Dryer heat polymerizes triglycerides into the cotton fiber, which is what turns "I can still see it" into "I will never get it out."

If the oil is still wet on the surface, press a folded white paper towel straight down on it. Lift, move to a clean section, press again. Repeat until no more transfers to the towel. Don't rub — rubbing smears oil sideways under pressure and usually makes the stain 2–3× bigger than it started.

Generously sprinkle cornstarch, baking soda, or unscented talcum powder over the stain. Let it sit 10 to 15 minutes (30 minutes on a heavy stain). The powder wicks oil out of the fiber before any wet chemistry goes in. You'll often see the powder turn translucent or gray where it pulled oil — that's exactly what you want. Brush off with a soft toothbrush or the edge of a credit card, then shake the item out.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn dish soap — work it in DRY
homeSolutions.instruction: Place a small drop of clear Dawn directly on the stain. Do not add water yet. Work it into the fiber with a fingertip or a soft toothbrush in gentle circles, then let it sit 10 minutes. Dawn is the household surfactant that wildlife rescuers use on oil-soaked birds — it is exceptional at lifting grease. Dry work matters here: water dilutes Dawn's grease-cutting power right when you want it at full strength.

Rinse with warm (not hot) water from the back of the fabric so you push the dissolved oil back out the way it came in rather than forward through the fiber. If a shadow is still visible, apply a second drop of Dawn, work it in dry for 5 more minutes, and rinse again. Heavy oil stains commonly need two passes — that is normal, not a sign of failure.

If any shadow persists after the Dawn work, pretreat with an enzyme stain remover — OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout — per the label instructions. Let it dwell 30 minutes. Then launder on warm (cotton's comfort zone) with your normal detergent plus a half-cup of white vinegar or an extra scoop of enzyme booster. Warm water dissolves residual oil that cold water cannot.

Hang or lay flat to air-dry. Do not tumble-dry until you have inspected the dry fabric in daylight. Even faint oil shadows will lock in permanently under dryer heat — this is the most common "I thought it was gone" mistake. If you see any shadow at all, repeat steps 4–6 before drying on heat.

homeSolutions.agent: Take it to a dry cleaner
homeSolutions.instruction: If the oil has already been through a hot dryer, is older than 72 hours, or survives two full home rounds, bring it to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Cooking oil on cotton, age [fresh / dry-cycled / old], treated at home with cornstarch preload + Dawn dry-work + [enzyme]. No heat applied at home." Pros use POG (paint-oil-grease solvent) and amyl acetate — true solvents that cleave polymerized oil — that are not safely available as household products.

steps: 1. Sprinkle baking soda or cornstarch on the stain to absorb surface oil — wait 10 min
2. Brush off powder with a clean cloth
3. Apply Dawn dish soap directly to the stain. Work gently into fabric
4. Rinse with warm water. Repeat soap application if stain persists
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer until stain is completely gone
products: enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after three treatment rounds. Take it to a professional dry cleaner — they have solvents (POG, amyl acetate) that actually cleave polymerized oil.
warnings: Never add water to a fresh oil stain first — water and oil don't mix, you will spread it
Dryer heat permanently sets residual oil

neverDo: No prolonged acid exposure on colored cotton
No chlorine bleach on colored cotton — strips dyes
No dryer heat until the stain is fully gone
fiberSensitivities: Cotton is the most chemically durable textile fiber
Test colorfastness on colored cotton before peroxide or amyl acetate

escalation.when: If cooking-oil stain persists after 2–3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is cooking oil on cotton, how old it is, and what products you already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches.

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the oil fresh or has it been washed/dried already?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
